[IMAGE:0]It's just before midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"Concentration is gentle, unstrained, effortless one-pointedness.  It is grounded and founded in the realization of the Law.  It may be interpreted as steadfastness of purpose. As a candle buring in a still night, so is realization burning steadfastly in the midst of concentration.  Concentration is the continuity of spiritual motion toward the One." 
 
That's from Manly P. Hall's Self-Unfoldment by Disciplines of Realization, arguably Hall's best book and one of the classics of the Golden Age of American occultism. (The image to the left, on the other hand, is one of Augustus Knapp's splendid illustrations for Hall's The Secret Teachings of All Ages.)     

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

There's a great story about JRR Tolkien. The first time somebody came to interview him with a tape recorder, he expressed quite a bit of suspicious curiosity about it and wanted to know how it worked. The reporter offered to record him saying something, and then play it back for him. Tolkien accepted with enthusiasm, and proceeded to belt out a few minutes of something in a language the reporter didn't understand at all. When the machine played it back, Tolkien said, "Good. That was the Lord's Prayer in Gothic; now I know there are no demons in that thing."

(In case you were wondering:

"Atta unsar, þu in himinam, weihnai namo þein,
qimai þiudinassus þeins, wairþai wilja þeins,
swe in himina jah ana airþai.
Hlaif unsarana þana sinteinan gif uns himma daga,
jah aflet uns þatei skulans sijaima,
swaswe jah weis afletam þaim skulam unsaraim,
jah ni briggais uns in fraistubnjai,
ak lausei uns af þamma ubilin;
unte þeina ist þiudangardi, jah mahts,
jah wulþus in aiwins. AMEN.")

replies.content:
Lady CK: that made me chuckle!

On well-off people living in horrible conditions: I've seen it, but since I no longer know anyone well-off, I can't say if it's changed at all. From what I've observed in the past, there are three things going on:

1) OCD: Obsessive-compulsive behaviors around germs tend to result in conditions that are far more unsanitary than you'd expect from someone afraid of germs. Just as washing your hands 100x/day leads to scabbed knuckles. I've seen this up-close and personal, and it's horrifying. If you can't use a hand-towel more than once, what do you do? Well, you dry your hands on three paper towels every time you wash your hands. 100x a day. Then your bathroom trash is overflowing all the time. When you get out of the shower, you drip on the floor a bit, and the paper towel heap gets wet. You don't take out that trash, because it's contaminated, and you don't want to touch it. So it mildews and sticks to the floor. Eventually, if you're lucky and you have roommates (ask me how I know about this...), they will put on dish gloves, clear out the trash for you, and probably stage an intervention. If you live alone... God help you. Multiply this by dirty dishes, pet waste, toilet scrubbing, kitchen trash, and everything else around the house that needs routine cleaning... yeah. Not being able to touch contaminated things means it's darn hard to get them cleaned up. And you can end up living in a reeking, vermin-infested, trash-piled-to-the-ceiling firetrap in less time than you ever thought possible.

2) Entitlement/inexperience. People who grew up with a cleaning lady who came twice a week, feel like they deserve to have that, and currently don't have one (finances, immigration enforcement, lockdowns, whatever) ... simply don't have the experience to manage basic household maintenance. How often do you change the sheets? What do you mean the floor needs sweeping every day? How do you even clean a bathroom? Why should I have to learn this? Combine this with the colossal task of maintaining a 2500+ sqft house... I'm not sure it's even possible.

3)Depression of any kind makes all of this exponentially worse, because now, there's no will to even *start*.

replies.content:
Ineresting. I was raised as a latchkey kid in the ghetto but going to private school with senators' and congressmen's kids. We were a very strange kind of urban poor. I don't remember any hunger or even lack of choice in what to eat. Everything was generic, but the pantry always had food. There was always frozen OJ in the freezer because I loved fruit, but buying all fresh fruit was too expensive.

Growing up, food never got left out because you don't want to have to fight off rats and roaches. As an adult, I have always loved having food visible — fruit, onions, and potatoes in bowls on the counter; nuts, grains, dried fruit, and crackers in mason jars on open shelves. The modern American obsession with sterile kitchens where everything is hidden behind doors makes me feel weird. Seeing healthy food around automatically leads me to eat more healthfully, but it also gives me a feeling of abundance and wealth.

When I used to make my own granola, the process of mixing bowls full of nuts and dried fruits was so satisfying. Now I feel so rich when I'm mixing up herbs to make teas. I wonder if that sense of satisfaction and riches from having mounds of food around comes from having less available in childhood, or from a previous life/death involving hunger? Or was I once a market merchant used to piling up trays of produce for sale? That's worth some meditation.

— Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hah! I grew up in a similar not-deprived-but... household. Parents were vegetarians, we were on a tight grocery budget, and Mom never had time to cook anything that took more than 20 minutes.

In adulthood, I learned to grill, roast, and slow-cook meat with a fantastic and wonderful sense of accomplishment. Also get a big boost out of making even really basic things that take a long time, such as baking bread, and making refried beans from overnight-soaked dry beans. It's hard to convey how much these skills mean to me.

My grandma lived through the great depression in fairly extreme poverty. She never hoarded food and her house was always neat as a pin, but we loved exploring her "filing system" in all the closets and cupboards. She carefully and neatly saved rubber bands, useful strings, bags, yogurt cups, scraps of fabric, pantyhose elastics, buttons, pins... she had a hundred ways to re-use these things. We always say she was an apex recycler, before recycling was even cool. Grandpa was similar: a whole workshop full of carefully sorted and labeled glass jars and cigar boxes, full of screws, nails, nuts, bolts, switches, locks, and other useful parts. I suspect a lot of people of their generation were similar.

I wonder what oddities we have, that our grandkids will wonder at when we are old...

Since the luminaries (Sun and Moon) are always direct, does that mean they always enjoy an accidental dignity, and should get a plus on their balance sheet, or should it not even be counted it in this case?

2. I’ve seen several attempts to “disprove” natal astrology by, for example, looking at placements that supposedly indicate high intelligence and correlating them to the IQ scores of random samples of people, and finding that there is no correlation. I assume that you wouldn’t consider this to be a valid approach? If not, could you explain why not?

3. For the past couple of months, I’ve been seriously considering becoming a worshiper of a certain love goddess. During this time, I haven’t performed any religious practices to gain the deity’s favor, such as prayer, sacrifice, ritual etc. However, I may have asked for the deity’s assistance, consciously or subconsciously, in helping me solve a love-related problem, and in the meantime, that problem appears to have been solved. My request for assistance was fairly simple, something to the effect of “Please help me with this problem,” which I assume is a standard formulation for most religious practitioners. Does the mere fact of asking, without any support in the form of concrete practices, bind me to the deity in any way, or create a debt that I’m bound to repay?

4. Does your recommended “standard diet” of magical practice (LBRP, MP, divination, meditation) affect a person’s aura or energy in any way? Will consistent practice of this routine affect others’ perception of you, and if so, in what ways?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Of course retrogradation is a debility. It's simply a debility that you can work with. (The same is true of every other debility, for that matter.) The Sun and Moon as luminaries are stronger than the planets anyway, and I suppose you could identify their lack of retrogradation as a factor in that.

2) That kind of single-factor analysis is by definition bad astrology. You have to look at the whole chart, not just the placement of this or that planet. Yes, this makes astrology very hard to analyze statistically. (The same is true of sciences such as ecology, which are a nightmare to analyze in quantitative terms.)

3) You've asked someone for a favor, and gotten it. Does that place you under an obligation? Well, would it if the person were human?

4) Of course -- that's much of the point of it. What effect it will have on other people depends very much on the people in question.

comments.title: Basic Skill Set For A Beginning Magican
comments.content:
Building on your series on the Will, what skills would you suggest someone would need to become proficient at before moving on to more advanced magical work?

I was thinking a general guideline would be something like:

1) Taking Charge of Your Physical, Mental and Spiritual Hygiene: You can't run a marathon if you are out of shape. You can't be clear headed if you have deep issues you need to address first. You can't deal with the Spirit World if you don't have a sense of the spiritual.

2) Learning to Meditate: Being able to sit and meditate on subjects with some success.

3) Developing Your Willpower: You need to be able to set tasks for yourself and stick to them. If you say, I will pray every morning and night before bed, that doesn't mean when ever you can get to it. If you say I will take a walk each day, it means walking in the rain sometimes. You should also be able to set simple goals for yourself and work towards them until they are done.

4) Be Able To Do Simple Visualization: Whether its simple things like imagining an energy ball in your hand, or more complicated things like imagining a ritual pentagram on fire in your mind, you need to be able to hold an image as if it were real.

5) Basic Divination: While you don't need to be able to do the whole Life reading, you should be able to get simple yes/no answers from the spirits.

6) Be Able To Protect Yourself and Your Workspace: You should know what herbs, stones and objects can be used to protect yourself and banish negative energy. This would include how to sanctify an area like a circle to work from.

7) Banish Spiritual Entities You Encounter: This might could have gone into #4, but you should know what prayers, items or basic rituals can remove a spirit you don't want near.

Any others you could recommend or suggest?

---

I suspect you'll say first, what kind of occult work would I want to do, beyond basic stuff, and I don't know. I also suspect you'll say read one of your books. I've looked over the Sop stuff but with the Golden Dawn system, I've got a weird reluctance to go that route. It might just be the "spinach affect" in that I don't like it until I try it.

replies.title: Re: Basic Skill Set For A Beginning Magican
replies.content: There I can help you. Start by picking up a copy of CS Lewis' book The Discarded Image and get familiar with the Renaissance world view. Follow it up with Plato, Aristotle, and Boethius -- you especially want to study Plato's Timaeus and Boethius' Consolation of Philosophy. Then study Lilly's Christian Astrology -- it's the most accessible source for classic astrology, which was the foundation of the old magical worldview. If you're comfortable with sacramental Christianity, you know where you need to be on Sunday mornings, too. Those should give you the grounding you need to fill in the rest of your magical education, and get started on the magical work.

There are three "directions" represented in the 24 different paths between the stations (stations marked by empty circles below): 1) from one station to the one immediately following it - around the circle, 8 paths - represented in yellow in the following image; 2) from one station to the one-next-over (skipping one station) - around the outermost squares, 8 paths - represented in orange in the following image; 3) from each of the eight stations to the inner (grove) circle - the rays, 8 paths - represented in scarlet and green in the following image (ignore the magenta paths and the numbered circles for now):

[IMAGE:1]

So, following how the fews proceed from path to path, I noticed an elegant pattern: an alternation from circle to square (multiplied) and then to the rays. However the assignment of one path disrupted this. Below you can see the progression, until you get to the last of the paths along the circle where instead of leading to the last of the square-lines, the motion jumps to the rays, proceeds through 4 of the rays, jumps back out to the remaining square-line, then jumps back in for the last 4 rays. In this image, the movements are numbered and you can see the "odd man out" that is Eadha at what is the 20th position (rather than the 16th) - both positions circled in yellow:

[IMAGE:2]

Had the progression proceeded without that abrupt switch, it would've tracked like this without a leap "in" toward the center then back "out" then back "in":

[IMAGE:3]

Finally my question - is there a logic behind placing Eadha's path (Samhuinn to Imbolc) in the position that might have logically gone to Ailm (since Ailm follows Ruis in the order of the fews and Ruis's path is the last one of the circle)?

Or is this likely just a case of someone back in the "lineage" said or drafted something and after that everyone went with it?

I admittedly didn't meditate on it too deeply (beyond just noticing that the fews stop being in order there, and having that send me off to track paths and draw pictures) - and this because I'm not following Ogham right now (viz. my previous week's comment about the elemental correspondences and my seeing just how closely the elemental combinations can track Ogham on the Wheel of the Year - current answer: not so well).

So, how to make sense of this if any is to be made...

replies.title: Re: Transits

1) Yes, the nature of conjunctions depends on the planet. It has one wrinkle -- the Sun, for example, harms any planet in conjunction with it; the planet is said to be "combust." Other than that, conjunctions with benefics are benefic and those with malefics are malefic.

2) That's good for Jupiter and all things ruled by him, but not good for the Moon and all things ruled by her. An afflicted planet can still give dignity to another planet. Equally, a well-dignified Saturn square another planet still afflicts that planet.

comments.title: Dreams and Literature
comments.content:
After your post on Hesse and the discussions afterwards I came to wonder whether anyone so far has seriously considered interpreting the content of books like dreams?

For some books - parts of Steppenwolf are a good example to my mind - this seems quite straightforward since they are very dreamlike in their imagery, for others not. But still, the images conjured in books come out of or through the authors imagination and should therefore originate at least partly on the astral plane (or sometimes maybe even above) where we "are" when we dream. There are two perspectives, obviously - to interpret the dream as if the author was the dreamer or to interpret it as if it was your own dream. I believe this makes possible a "classification scheme" for literature ranging from junk to written myths. Like you can have mental-noise-every-day-lower-astral-dreams which contain little information at all, vivid, profound and highly symbolic dreams which stand out in their importance for you as an individual or even dreams going beyond your own individuality tapping into the collective unconscious or even contact with the divine. This in turn hints that there are ways (obviously there are) to prepare oneself to experience dreams that go beyond or even far beyond mental noise. Despite not being so much into dream work, do you know / can you recommend any written resources in this regard?

Thank you and have a nice monday!
Nachtgurke

comments.content:
Iamblicus described daemons as the manifestation of the gods or will of the gods into corporeal form or laws.

That evil is simply an ignorance of the laws and so being run over by otherwise neutral (or benevolent) forces.

How does this relate to the idea of malevelent supernatural entities or "demons" that you speak of?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The daemons Iamblichus talked about are not demons in the modern sense of the word, and his concept of evil was not as narrow as you've suggested. In Neoplatonism evil is understood as the absence of an appropriate good. Virtues are the different forms of good expressed in individual beings; there are beings who lack their appropriate virtues; if they lack enough of the virtues that are proper to them, it's reasonable to call them evil beings, since they are beings in which good is largely absent. Malevolent spirits are evil beings in this sense. (So, of course, are malevolent humans.)

The late John Gardner, author of Grendel and a lot of other very good fiction, used to remind his writing students that they should always remember that someday, the person who would be reading their novels might be trying to decide whether or not to commit suicide -- and might be swayed one way or the other by what they're reading. Unfortunately that very sensible way of thinking has not caught on in fashionable literature, so much of which wallows in ugliness and despair.

I'd like to invoke Mnemosyne in the west for my SOP and I'm including "titles" or epithets for the deities I've added so far, I've searched for* but been unable to find more for Mnemosyne than "Mother of Muses." Do you or any readers know of other terms of address or phrases associated with her?

replies.title: Re: Mnemosyne
replies.content:
You might take a good translation of the Orphic Hymn to her, and turn one of the phrases there back into a title. I say "back" because in the original Greek, expressions that we render in English by complex clauses are often just compound nouns or adjectives that serve as titles or epithets.

It might take some work to make these less clumsy (ask her daughters' help with that!), but some starting points that spring to my mind from the hymn are:

"You who bring coherence to the mind and soul of mortals"
"You who increase men's/our/my ability and power to think"
"You who remind us of all the thoughts we store in our breast"

1st mother: Puer
2nd mother: Caput Draconis
3rd mother: Laetitia
4th mother: Populus
1st daughter: Amissio
2nd daughter: Fortuna Minor
3rd daughter: Rubeus

1st niece: Amissio
2nd niece: Laetitia
3rd niece: Conjunctio
4th niece: Laetitia
Right Witness: Albus

Judge: Fortuna Minor
